text.title: Optimization Cycle: Search Terms, Actions, and Schedule
text.summary: Weekly search terms triage process, optimization actions by symptom, budget reallocation logic, and reporting cadence (daily/weekly/monthly).

section.title: Search Terms Triage

rule.statement: Review the Search Terms report weekly; filter by cost above $5 and classify each term as: relevant+converting (add as keyword), relevant+non-converting (monitor), irrelevant (add as negative).
rule.rationale: A weekly search-terms triage is the highest-ROI optimization action in Google Ads. Irrelevant clicks waste budget on impossible conversions. Adding high-converting terms as keywords improves Quality Score.

antipattern.description: Skipping search terms review for more than 2 weeks.
antipattern.reason: Search term drift accumulates rapidly. After 2 weeks without triage, a budget-hungry irrelevant query can consume 10-20% of total spend before anyone notices.

section.reference: GAQL helper that pulls search terms with cost above threshold and zero conversions for negative keyword candidates

list.item: Low impressions: increase budget, broaden match types, review bid floors
Low CTR: rewrite headlines, tighten ad groups, test new value props
Low Quality Score: tighten keyword-to-ad copy match, improve landing page relevance
Low conversion rate: optimize landing page, verify conversion tracking fires
High CPA: add negatives, reduce bids on poor segments, improve quality
Low impression share: increase budget (if budget-lost), increase bids (if rank-lost)

rule.statement: Make major structural changes (bid strategy, campaign restructure, audience rebuild) no more than once a month; each major change resets Smart Bidding learning.
rule.rationale: Smart Bidding requires 7-14 days after significant changes to restabilize. Frequent major changes keep campaigns in perpetual learning and prevent stable performance.

rule.statement: Reallocate budget from campaigns over CPA target with high impression share to campaigns under CPA target with low impression share.
rule.rationale: A campaign performing well below target with low impression share is constrained by budget, not efficiency. Shifting budget there captures more high-value conversions at a lower cost than fixing an over-target campaign.

example.note: Campaign A: $100/day, CPA $20 (target $25), 80% impression share — already saturated; hold spend. Campaign C: $50/day, CPA $15 (target $25), 40% impression share — increase to $100/day. More conversions at lower CPA from additional budget.

list.item: Daily (5 min): check spend pacing, look for anomalies, ensure no disapprovals
Weekly (30 min): full performance review, search terms triage, add negatives, bid adjustments, Quality Score review
Monthly (1 hour): trend analysis, competitive review via Auction Insights, budget reallocation, strategy assessment

rule.statement: Always compare period-over-period AND year-over-year; seasonal effects mask real performance changes when only looking at 7-day vs. prior-7-day.
rule.rationale: A 20% CPA increase in December vs. November may simply reflect seasonal demand shifts. Year-over-year comparison reveals whether the change is a real efficiency problem or expected seasonality.
